spec_match.rationale: anchor-scope: --target on the list confines anchor-name lookups inside each list, exactly the spec property.

interactive.rationale: Toggle to flip anchor-scope on/off works. But hovering rows runs a JS hack that strips anchor-name from siblings — the demo's 'badge follows hover' behaviour comes from JS, NOT from CSS scoping. That undersells the spec, and conflates which mechanism the visitor is seeing.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb says 'hover any row to anchor the badge' but the badge follows the hover via JS, not via CSS anchor-name on the hovered row. The promise of pure CSS resolution is muddled.

edge_cases.rationale: Demo doesn't actually visualise the WITHOUT-scope failure mode clearly — both badges still appear roughly in the same place because of the JS hack, so flipping the toggle off doesn't produce the dramatic collision the visitor expects.

references.rationale: Direct chromestatus and spec link present.

design_system.rationale: CSS variables, no static-card, badge uses --accent-blue/--bg-ivory (passes WCAG AA).

openQuestions.title: Remove the JS anchor-name override
openQuestions.detail: The JS sets `row.style.anchorName = '--target'` on hover and strips it from siblings — that defeats the purpose of demonstrating anchor-scope. Drop the JS hack, give EVERY row `anchor-name: --target` permanently in CSS, and let the badge land where CSS resolution puts it. With scope off, both badges should pile on the same row; with scope on, each list's badge should attach to a single row inside that list.

openQuestions.title: Make the without-scope failure dramatic
openQuestions.detail: Currently the toggle off doesn't visibly break anything because of the JS hack. Once removed, the off state should clearly show both badges stacking on the same physical row (probably the last row in the DOM). Add a callout explaining what the visitor is seeing.

summary: The toggle is real but the underlying behaviour is JS-driven, so anchor-scope itself doesn't actually animate the badge motion in this demo. Remove the JS hack so the CSS-only contrast lands.
